factual-accuracy.requirement: States that in a 2019 cross-sectional survey of Rohingya refugee women in Camp-4, Cox’s Bazar, approximately 71.6% of respondents reported attending at least four antenatal care visits (ANC4+)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports that a cross-sectional study in Camp-4 found 71.57% had at least four ANC visits, but it dates this study to 2025 rather than 2019. It therefore does not state that a 2019 Camp-4 survey found approximately 71.6% ANC4+.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that in a retrospective analysis of Thailand–Myanmar border camp maternal health data through 2018 (including Mae La), more than 90% of pregnant women attended ≥4 antenatal care visits (ANC4+)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that a peer-reviewed retrospective analysis of Thai–Myanmar border camps covering 2000–2018 found “more than 90% of pregnant women attended four or more antenatal care visits,” and it explicitly references Mae La in the Mae La section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Rohingya facility births/SBA started at 12-22% in 2018 (or as low as 4% in late 2017 emergency period)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that skilled birth attendance in Rohingya camps rose “from 12%” in 2018, which matches the required 12–22% starting range for 2018. It does not mention the alternative “as low as 4% in late 2017 emergency period,” but the 2018 baseline requirement is satisfied.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States at least three distinct year–percentage pairs documenting facility-based delivery among Rohingya refugees in Cox’s Bazar, including one estimate ≤15% in 2018, one intermediate estimate between 40–60% from 2019–2020, and one estimate ≥75% from 2022–2023
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes year–percentage pairs for facility-based delivery in Cox’s Bazar: 22% in February 2018 (not ≤15%), 67% in April–June 2021 (outside 2019–2020), and “over 95% in 2022” (≥75%). It does not provide an estimate ≤15% in 2018 nor an intermediate 40–60% estimate specifically from 2019–2020.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La facility births/SBA at 75-80% with stable mature service delivery
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports Mae La skilled birth attendance at 95% and elsewhere mentions 80% SBA in other camps, but it never states Mae La facility births/SBA specifically at 75–80% nor characterizes it as “stable mature service delivery.” The required 75–80% figure and the stability/maturity framing are absent for Mae La.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Rohingya NMR as 27 per 1,000 live births for September 2017-December 2018 period with most deaths occurring at home
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that for September 2017–December 2018 the Rohingya neonatal mortality rate was 27.0 per 1,000 live births and adds that 76.3% of neonatal deaths occurred at home or in the community.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La NMR declined to approximately 10-11 per 1,000 live births by 2011 and remained stable
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Mae La neonatal mortality declining from 21.8 to 10.7 per 1,000 live births between 2009 and 2011, but it does not state that it declined to approximately 10–11 per 1,000 by 2011 and remained stable thereafter. There is no explicit claim of post-2011 stability for Mae La NMR.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Rohingya MMR approximately 200-230 per 100,000 live births in 2022-2023 period (accounting for methodology variation between 200 and 295)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports Rohingya maternal mortality as “near 300 per 100,000 live births” (Sept 2023 bulletin) and summarizes Cox’s Bazar MMR as “179–300 per 100,000,” but it does not state an approximate 200–230 range for 2022–2023 or explicitly note methodology variation spanning 200 to 295.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that the maternal mortality ratio among Rohingya women in Cox’s Bazar refugee camps was reported at 295 per 100,000 live births in 2023 according to health sector reporting
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions a September 2023 Health Sector Bulletin estimating maternal mortality “near 300 per 100,000 live births,” but it does not state that the maternal mortality ratio was reported at exactly 295 per 100,000 live births in 2023. It also does not attribute a 295 figure specifically to “health sector reporting” for 2023.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La or SMRU Thai-resident refugee cohorts had low MMR (~79-126 per 100,000) with zero malaria-related maternal deaths since 2005
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports Mae La/SMRU maternal mortality ratios around 126–176 per 100,000, but it does not state the lower ~79 figure nor does it state that there have been zero malaria-related maternal deaths since 2005. The specific claim about “zero malaria-related maternal deaths since 2005” is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that obstetric hemorrhage (including postpartum hemorrhage) is reported as a leading direct cause category of maternal deaths in the Rohingya Cox’s Bazar MPMSR surveillance reports, and states that postpartum hemorrhage contributes to maternal deaths in Thailand–Myanmar border refugee/migrant settings
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that obstetric hemorrhage is a leading cause category of maternal deaths in Cox’s Bazar MPMSR reports (e.g., 2020, 2022, 2023 MPMSR sections). It also states that postpartum hemorrhage is commonly reported as a cause of maternal mortality in Thailand–Myanmar border refugee/migrant settings (Mae La section citing Ibis Reproductive Health and an intrapartum care study).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States routine PPH incidence per 1,000 births is not publicly reported or available for either setting
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses postpartum hemorrhage mainly as a leading cause of maternal death and notes difficulty comparing PPH incidence, but it never states that routine PPH incidence per 1,000 births is not publicly reported or unavailable for either Cox’s Bazar or Mae La. It also provides no explicit statement about non-availability for both settings.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that health care in the Cox’s Bazar Rohingya camps is at least 2 of the following: delivered through a coordinated humanitarian partner system (WHO-led Health Sector with NGO/UN partners), includes primary health care centers, includes field hospitals, or explicitly notes the presence of 24/7 PHCC services.
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Cox’s Bazar healthcare is delivered through a coordinated Health Sector with WHO/UN/NGO partners and describes a network including primary health care centers and secondary care field hospitals. It also explicitly notes 24/7 services (e.g., MSF’s 24/7 facility and UNFPA-supported Hope Field Hospital as a 24/7 CEmONC provider).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes Mae La as NGO primary care (SMRU, PU-AMI, or Malteser) integrated with Thai Ministry of Public Health for referrals
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Mae La’s model as NGO-implemented primary care with Thai Ministry of Public Health oversight and integration for surveillance/vaccination and, crucially, referrals to Thai hospitals (e.g., Tha Song Yang Hospital, Mae Sot General Hospital). It also names NGO providers including SMRU and Malteser International as key health providers in Mae La.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Cox's Bazar has >400 midwives active in response or approximately 213 midwives at UNFPA facilities during 2021-2022
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that “as of March 2024… 466 midwives in the camps” and also notes UNFPA supports “100–300 midwives deployed in the camps.” This satisfies the requirement to state Cox’s Bazar has >400 midwives active in the response.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States at least one of the following: that no published peer-reviewed/authoritative reports provide a midwife-to-population ratio for Cox’s Bazar refugee camps or host communities, OR explicitly avoids reporting a numeric ratio for midwives in this setting
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports explicit numeric midwife-to-population ratios for Cox’s Bazar (e.g., “1 midwife per 2,067 people”) rather than stating that no authoritative published ratio exists or avoiding numeric ratios. It does not include any disclaimer that peer-reviewed/authoritative sources do not provide such a ratio for Cox’s Bazar camps or host communities.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La has approximately 20 camp-resident SBAs or describes small stable established SBA workforce mentored by SMRU
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state that Mae La has approximately 20 camp-resident skilled birth attendants, nor does it describe a small stable established SBA workforce mentored by SMRU. It mentions SMRU training and provides staffing figures like “72 qualified nurses” and “26 midwives” in other camps, but not the specific ~20 camp-resident SBAs point.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes Cox's Bazar EmONC access including on-camp Friendship CEmONC hospital and UNFPA referral hubs launched 2019-2020 providing 24/7 transport
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Cox’s Bazar EmONC generally (e.g., Hope Field Hospital, referral hubs/ambulances) but does not mention the on-camp Friendship CEmONC hospital or specify UNFPA referral hubs launched in 2019–2020 providing 24/7 transport. Those named elements and the 2019–2020 launch timeframe are absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La requires referral to Thai MoPH hospitals for C-sections at distance of 60-80 km or typically under 2 hours travel
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes Mae La C-sections are referred to Tha Song Yang Hospital (~57 km, ~56 minutes) and mentions an 80 km referral taking 3.5–7 hours, but it does not state that Mae La requires referral to Thai MoPH hospitals for C-sections at a distance of 60–80 km or typically under 2 hours travel. The specific 60–80 km/under-2-hours framing is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States TBAs in both settings transitioned from delivery providers to mediators, companions, or community mobilizers rather than clinical attendants
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes in Cox’s Bazar that TBAs were trained to refer women to facilities rather than conduct home deliveries, and in Mae La that TBAs were engaged as cultural mediators via mother support groups and training programs. It explicitly frames TBAs in both settings as referral agents/cultural mediators rather than clinical attendants.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes 2021 UNFPA funding impact was mixed or limited evidence with services continuing to improve, and more acute disruptions documented in 2023-2025 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes the 2021 UNFPA funding reduction as having a direct, immediate impact in Cox’s Bazar while Mae La showed more resilience, but it does not characterize the 2021 impact as mixed/limited evidence with services continuing to improve. It also does not explicitly contrast this with more acute, better-documented disruptions specifically in the 2023–2025 period as required.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Shows at least 3 intermediate time points for key indicator progression (e.g., facility births 32%→52.7%→60%→82%) not just baseline and endpoint
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes multiple intermediate time points for indicator progression, such as skilled birth attendance in Cox’s Bazar (12% in 2018 → 67% in 2021 → >80% in 2024 → 88% in 2024/93% in 2023) and facility births in the CPI catchment (33% in 2020 → >95% in 2022).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly explains how specific inputs (midwife scale-up, referral hubs) drove outcome changes rather than merely correlating them
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response goes beyond correlation by describing mechanisms such as UNFPA midwife deployment increasing skilled attendance, TBA training shifting behavior toward facility referral (33%→95% facility births), and referral/transport delays affecting hemorrhage-related mortality. It also links limited in-camp CEmONC and referral journeys to outcomes rather than only listing associations.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Addresses apparent contradictions in source data and explains why different figures appear (methodology, scope, or temporal differences)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly flags conflicting figures (e.g., UNHCR reporting 93% skilled birth attendance vs WHO monitoring suggesting 43% facility births) and notes “data consistency” and “methodological challenges” as reasons. It also explains that some apparent increases may reflect improved surveillance rather than true outcome worsening, addressing temporal/method differences.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Clearly separates 2017-2023 data from post-period updates and flags when cited figures fall outside requested timeframe
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly includes and relies on figures from 2024–2026 (e.g., 2024 bulletins, 2025 studies, 2026 hospital opening) without clearly separating them into a distinct “post-2023 updates” section or consistently flagging them as outside the 2017–2023 timeframe. While it states the analysis covers 2017–2023, it does not systematically label out-of-window statistics when presenting comparisons.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains how specific delivery model features (parallel vs integrated, CEmONC location, referral design) produce different outcome patterns
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response links delivery-model features to outcomes by contrasting Cox’s Bazar’s NGO-run, multi-partner parallel system with limited in-camp CEmONC and complex referrals versus Mae La’s MOPH-integrated referral arrangements and more stable staffing, and it connects these to patterns in ANC/SBA, maternal causes (hemorrhage), and neonatal mortality. It also discusses how referral distance/2-hour access and TBA cultural mediation approaches plausibly drive different outcome profiles.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies EmONC signal functions available, facility counts/types, or referral pathway details beyond general model description
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response goes beyond general model description by detailing EmOC availability and referral pathways (e.g., only 1 active CEmONC facility in March 2024, six-hour multi-facility referral journeys, MoA-based referrals to Tha Song Yang/Mae Sot hospitals with distances/times). It also specifies facility counts/types in Cox’s Bazar (health posts, PHCCs, field hospitals) and notes in-camp vs referral-based CEmONC in Mae La.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Questions or qualifies the 2021 funding reduction claim with evidence rather than accepting premise, noting what data support or contradict it
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response largely accepts the premise that the 2021 UNFPA funding reduction had direct impacts, asserting it was “direct and immediate” in Cox’s Bazar and describing effects without questioning attribution. It does not explicitly qualify the claim by weighing UNHCR HIS/MSF evidence that would support or contradict a causal link in both locations.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Treats Cox's Bazar parallel humanitarian system and Mae La integrated Thai MoPH model as functionally equivalent without distinguishing their structural differences
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly contrasts Cox’s Bazar as an NGO-coordinated, multi-partner field-hospital/parallel system with Mae La as a Thai MoPH-integrated/oversight model with formal referral linkages, and it highlights differences like in-camp CEmONC availability and governance. It does not present the two systems as functionally equivalent.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents health metrics with denominators, time periods, and appropriate qualifiers (e.g., "per 1,000 live births," "2017-2023")
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly reports metrics with explicit denominators and time periods, e.g., maternal mortality “per 100,000 live births,” neonatal mortality “per 1,000 live births,” and frames the analysis as covering “2017–2023,” with additional year-specific qualifiers (e.g., 2018, 2022, Sept 2023, March 2024).

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses systematic side-by-side comparison format for settings/indicators with clear headings or tables
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple side-by-side comparison tables with clear headings (e.g., “Comparative Assessment of Delivery Models,” “Comparative Analysis of Cultural Mediation Approaches,” “Comparative Funding Impact Assessment,” and the final indicator summary table) to systematically compare the two settings and indicators.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains scholarly analytical tone and explicitly flags data gaps, methodological variations, or uncertainty in evidence
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, report-like analytical tone and repeatedly flags uncertainty and methodological/data gaps (e.g., conflicting UNHCR vs WHO facility-birth figures, difficulty comparing PPH incidence due to surveillance differences, and notes about improved surveillance affecting MMR trends). It explicitly highlights data consistency and measurement challenges across sources.

presentation-quality.requirement: Repeats the same health indicator, statistic, or key finding in more than two separate sections without adding new context
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeats the same key indicators (e.g., ANC 4+ rates, skilled birth attendance, neonatal mortality, and EmOC/CEmONC availability) across multiple sections—detailed in Section 2, revisited again in Sections 5–6, and summarized again in Section 7—often restating the same figures without materially new context.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents Cox's Bazar vs Mae La indicator comparisons in narrative paragraphs only, with no tables, bullet lists, or structured side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes multiple structured comparison tables (e.g., “Comparative Assessment of Delivery Models,” “Comparative Analysis of Cultural Mediation Approaches,” and summary indicator tables) and also uses bullet lists throughout. Therefore it is not presented in narrative paragraphs only.

citation-quality.requirement: References UNHCR iRHIS (integrated Refugee Health Information System) dashboards, camp-level health data, or UNHCR health surveillance systems
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references UNHCR Health Information System (HIS) site reports for Mae La (e.g., “UNHCR HIS 2018 report”) and uses UNHCR health surveillance outputs like the UNHCR Annual Public Health Global Reviews.

citation-quality.requirement: References MSF (Médecins Sans Frontières) operational reports, field observations, or humanitarian program documentation
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references MSF operational documentation, including an MSF source link (“MSF - Saving Lives That Have Just Begun in Cox's Bazar”) and describes MSF-run facilities and service statistics (e.g., births assisted, facility details) as field/program observations.

citation-quality.requirement: References MPMSR (Maternal and Perinatal Death Surveillance and Response) audits, WHO/MPMSR mortality reviews, or maternal death surveillance data
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references UNFPA MPMSR annual reports (2020, 2022, 2023) and uses MPMSR maternal death surveillance data (counts and causes of maternal deaths) in the postpartum hemorrhage/maternal mortality section.

citation-quality.requirement: References UNFPA operational reports, facility-level data, maternal health program documentation, or reproductive health service statistics
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple UNFPA MPMSR annual reports (2020, 2022, 2023) and uses UNHCR HIS facility-level reporting for Mae La (UNHCR HIS Site Report 2018), along with reproductive health service statistics such as ANC coverage, skilled birth attendance, and obstetric emergency caseloads.

citation-quality.requirement: References SMRU (Shoklo Malaria Research Unit) research publications, Thai border camp health program data, or Mae La clinical cohort studies
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references SMRU multiple times, including “SMRU cohort” pregnancy studies and SMRU birth attendant training, and cites an SMRU cohort profile publication as a source.

citation-quality.requirement: References household surveys, camp-specific assessments, or population-based data collection (e.g., KAP surveys, coverage surveys)
citation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly cites population-based and camp-specific data sources, including UNHCR Health Information System (HIS) site reports, MPMSR surveillance reports, and cross-sectional studies/cohort analyses used to estimate ANC coverage, skilled birth attendance, and neonatal mortality in the camps.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites only news articles, blog posts, or promotional materials as primary evidence for epidemiological indicators without surveillance data or operational reports
citation-quality.explanation: The response relies heavily on surveillance and operational sources (e.g., UNHCR HIS site report, WHO Health Sector Bulletins, UNFPA MPMSR surveillance reports, and MSF operational materials), not only news/blog/promotional items. While it includes some media and NGO web pages (e.g., The Guardian, Global Citizen), these are not the sole primary evidence base.
